title: Compiler and runtime diagnostics

canonicalExplanation.summary: Compile diagnostics describe source or type failures before execution, uncaught exceptions report runtime failure with a causal stack, and logic defects require contract-based observation despite successful execution.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A type mismatch is repaired from the first relevant javac diagnostic, while a later NullPointerException is traced from the exception type through the first application frame. Your goal is to classify and repair representative compile-time and runtime failures. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; First source and main. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Compile diagnostics describe source or type failures before execution, uncaught exceptions report runtime failure with a causal stack, and logic defects require contract-based observation despite successful execution. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: One syntax or type mistake can cascade into later diagnostics, stack traces show call locations rather than an automatic root-cause verdict, and clean output is not proof of correctness. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Every Java defect becomes either a compiler error or a stack trace, so a run without either must be correct.

includes: Separate compile errors, exceptions and logic failures and use stack-trace locations.
Classify and repair representative compile-time and runtime failures.
excludes: One syntax or type mistake can cascade into later diagnostics, stack traces show call locations rather than an automatic root-cause verdict, and clean output is not proof of correctness.

outcomes: Classify and repair representative compile-time and runtime failures.

examples.title: Compiler and runtime diagnostics: direct language case
examples.situation: A type mismatch is repaired from the first relevant javac diagnostic, while a later NullPointerException is traced from the exception type through the first application frame.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A type mismatch is repaired from the first relevant javac diagnostic, while a later NullPointerException is traced from the exception type through the first application frame.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Classify compile-time, exceptional runtime, and logic failures separately; start with the first causal diagnostic or relevant application stack frame and verify the repaired contract.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Classify compile-time, exceptional runtime, and logic failures separately; start with the first causal diagnostic or relevant application stack frame and verify the repaired contract.

examples.title: Compiler and runtime diagnostics: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A program compiles and exits normally but returns an off-by-one result, so a boundary assertion—not the compiler—reveals the logic defect.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A program compiles and exits normally but returns an off-by-one result, so a boundary assertion—not the compiler—reveals the logic defect.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: One syntax or type mistake can cascade into later diagnostics, stack traces show call locations rather than an automatic root-cause verdict, and clean output is not proof of correctness.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Every Java defect becomes either a compiler error or a stack trace, so a run without either must be correct.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Classify and repair representative compile-time and runtime failures.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every Java defect becomes either a compiler error or a stack trace, so a run without either must be correct.
misconceptions.correction: Classify compile-time, exceptional runtime, and logic failures separately; start with the first causal diagnostic or relevant application stack frame and verify the repaired contract.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Every Java defect becomes either a compiler error or a stack trace, so a run without either must be correct.

analogy.example: a workshop that turns a checked design into a labeled machine part and then runs it on a compatible line can introduce Compiler and runtime diagnostics, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.

limitations: Java compatibility, verification, class loading, and diagnostics follow formal specifications rather than physical manufacturing
